Hello ladies and gentlemen of the Dan Kennedy Business of Copywriting Academy. Welcome back to your Post-Academy Call with Dan and Katie. Katie, I know we've got a lot of questions so I'm happy to turn this over to you. Okay, great. Well, hi Dan. Hi. And hi everybody. It seems like it's been a little bit longer than just these two months. But everything's flown by here and I've been getting such great notes and questions from everybody and really appreciate it. And I just couldn't be more excited about the success that we're seeing that you've made happen. And I did just want to start off with two notes that came in recently. One from Alan Heissig. I hope I'm pronouncing that correctly. He sent a very nice note to Dan that I just want to share with everybody. He says, the business of Copywriting Academy was awesomely beneficial. It illuminated the factors and rationale which underpin my success roadmap. I put together a six-month, two-year, and five-year plan which I am executing and continuously updating. I have defined and concentrated on my niche, business to government, communications for small businesses providing contracted services, using Dan's analysis and reasoning for choosing client types. I just started working on my first major multi-month project. Congratulations Al. That's fabulous. I use Dan's philosophies in handling potential clients to engender a strong emotional dependency toward gaining my services. It works. By the way, I use the uniquely qualified expert reference methodology of the type discussed by Dan in order to uncover the client in the first place. That's just fabulous. He goes on. I'll keep going here because this really is inspiring. I've completed the draft of my first book that I conceived at the Academy. I have it out for a peer review and I'm working on the graphics at this time. I have already used the fact that I am writing a book to my advantage prior to publication. It has in turn already suggested potential follow on books. I modeled my book after the Adam Whitty book, 21 Ways to Build Your Business with a Book, which I received at the Academy and modeled the chapter titling approach after Dan's galley proof of his upcoming No BS Wealth Attraction in the New Economy, which gives you a little critical praise there that says it's dynamite. In keeping with my roadmap, I have started to lay out the groundwork for future steps. Here's what's going to happen to Alan very soon. He's going to become a public speaker on an aspect of my offering and turn elements of my offering into a training program for the employees of my intended clients. My clients are the business owners who control money rather than those who will take the courses on behalf of their business owners. Define and cultivate my own herd. The bottom line, I have started to weave together the elements to make a business and I have started to government offering fabric for a market niche that is fragmented and ill-defined. The underlying core competency is copywriting. The business foundation is Dan Kennedy. Cheers! He doesn't have any questions. That's all Alan had to say. That's fabulous. We got this note also from Tammy Call. She didn't think she was going to be able to make this call today because she's heading out of town. I've got my dentist coach to go to contract and agree to partner on writing a book together that will parlay into individualized marketing programs for using different types of media. She went back and got some things straightened out with them and is obviously in a much better position than she was in with them beforehand. All good. Now, what about Tammy's question about the people who want to work on a handshake? Everybody that would prefer to work on a handshake, that's because they're on the side of paying, not being paid. I agree. I mean, that's unacceptable. And it's as much, I mean, the argument for it to the degree that an argument has to be made is that documentation benefits both client and advisor, consultant, copywriter in preventing misunderstandings that jeopardize a relationship all later. And it has to be in place before work begins. I completely agree. Okay, I'm going to move on to Ben's question. This is Ben Heyman. Dan, you mentioned the importance of having a client induction handling process in educating the client in the rules of the road. How much of the process is managed by your personal assistant, Vicki? Does she do all the steps? And at what point do you get involved? This has varied a little bit off and on over the years. Currently, when somebody new surfaces, which fortunately this week we're dealing with two of those, she handles their first inquiry and explains to them how the process works and asks them to put together their thoughts and what they're looking for. And then it sort of depends. She will handle the next steps if it's somebody I'm not particularly excited about and think that it's pretty improbable that we're going to go anywhere. If it's somebody that appears to be really a good fit, I will read it out. I will respond to their memo with some detail in writing. I will make clear what fees both initial and project ranges look like. So if I'm really way outside their realm of ability to pay, we're going to make that goal. We're going to stop early. And then I drive them back to her to schedule either the initial consultation day or a phone appointment. And in about 75% of the time, so it's a question, is Dan only speak to the client once a fee has been issued? In about 75% of the time, yeah, I'm not doing anything but that memo and I'm not having any conversation with them until there's a check in the office. In about 25% of the time, I'm having a brief preliminary phone conversation with them before they're going to the initial day. And by brief, I mean 15 to 20 minutes tops. Okay. Well, I think that's going to answer Ben's questions. Now, Kelly wants to know, who was your first client and what was your process to get them? Well, when I saw that, I guess it's... I love the Gary Halbert story, is that it? Yeah, it kind of depends on how you characterize clients. Yeah, I mean, one of... I think the first thing I got paid to copyright was Halbert's, not directly from Gary, but on a beat-the-control call. My first real client of any significance was a national publishing company located in my area that I found only because... Again, I don't do it much, well, I don't do it at all now, but at the time, I read pretty much all the ads in a newspaper. And they actually... the client was actually running a help wanted ad for a copywriter and marketing person. And his description in the ad of his company, they were right up my alley. And so I rather clumsily called and said, you really don't want to hire anybody because I can't be hired, but you know, but you want me. And got to a meeting. The first really sizable client was a group, I mean, by six fake figures, was a group of chiropractic clinics that came out of a speaking gig. What might be instructive, I guess, is that in every one of these cases, there was a reason for they and I to be a fit beyond me just wanting to make money and write copy. I mean, there was some logical, sane rationale based on skill, expertise, interest, things I was already doing that made me a good fit for these clients. And so even in these early cases, a fairly cold prospecting, there was a rationale for the contact beyond just have Penn will work for food. Okay, well good. And Kelly did add to her note that the Academy was fantastic and I walked away with many aha moments and the confidence to take action. And I've spoken to Kelly just recently and I know she is taking action, so happy to hear that. Anthony wants to know, with regard to page 189, the Breakthrough Idea Discovery Process, please elaborate a bit more on what you do to look for the sweet spot. Yeah, and then there's a corresponding, forget who it's from, Chuck Dulce, Dulce. Oh, right. Yeah, right. And so he asked about subcultures and list biases and really one is almost the answer to the other. Okay, right. I mean, the what exists in just about every business somewhere is one or more customer biases where there is a particular customer within their customers who is a better fit for them than all the rest. And is going to be much more responsive than the big broad universe that they're marketing to if we can identify them and we can get directly to them. Sometimes this is pretty simple. It might just be age, it might be gender, it might be geography. The best customers are in a small town, but big city. It might be timing, X number of months before a wedding after a divorce. It might be even more complicated where it really speaks to psychographics, to attitude more than to demographics. So political philosophy, church going, not church going on and on and on. And when you find one that you can validate and you can somehow exploit, you have enormous leverage because you can narrow the field of who they market to and you can better craft the copy to directly address that person rather than a broader range of people. And so you immediately move up a number of notches in terms of your ability to produce really good results. So finding these things and very few clients are going to know them. Most of them are going to have a lot of the information you might need, but they're not really going to understand this and they won't have done it. So it's detective work. The process involves asking a lot of questions and pressing for information on who is, because the usual answer to who is your customer is like, well, everybody, you know, or everybody between the ages of 20 and 80 who owns a house. And so you really got to press beyond those kinds of answers and ask and ask and ask and ask. And then usually you got to force the client to go with you and hunt and look and dig. I had a client years ago in the, find a foreign bride for Guy's business. And so his starting answer was, you know, my customer is a guy between ages of 40 and 110. And typically he's been divorced two or more times and so he's had it with American women and he has an idealized vision in his head of, you know, a more compliant and cooperative person from Asia or Russia or whatever. And I said, well, you know, there's got to be some other biases. Wow. You know, gee, look here in our testimonial book. I mean, there's ministers and there's school teachers and there's truck drivers and there's executives and there's CEOs and there's, so we know the occupations. We know the occupation of everybody. Well, when you go and now get all the forms and you dig through them and stick count them yourself, overwhelming bias for long haul truck drivers. Now, once you know that once you have that piece of information, first of all, there's media that they read and that media isn't even being used. They're finding him because he's in USA today. Well, there's media that just reaches truck drivers and truck stops. So it changes the media equation. Furthermore, it dramatically affects the copy because now you're taking what was generic copy that was working and rewriting it to be truck drivers speak and talk to the long haul truck driver and you're chucking all the rest of the testimonials and filling the thing up with truck driver testimonials. Well, you're going to get much better response than he was getting with a generic campaign. And I'm almost, it's very rare that I can't find these when I hunt. And sometimes to hunt easy and sometimes, you know, the hunt is hard. That's gold, everybody. I really hope that that sinks in because I do believe that that is true for any possible product that you could be working on. I think that's very valuable advice. You know, I'm doing a lot of work right now for a anti-aging skin skincare company. And we're about to test taking the same list they've been mailing. Hopefully better copy. But mailing within those lists only those at benchmark birthdays, 40, 45, 50, 55, 60 and 665. Because there's abundant research in cosmetic surgery to indicate that. Sure. That's a big driver. And so, you know, it affects the copy, but it also gives the copy a much better chance to work. But it's a lot of, you know, Dan, I think what you're saying here, though, is that, you know, you're the one who does that work. You're the one that does that thinking. You have to. Yeah, they're not going to do it on their own. And some are going to realize that what you're doing is smart, miraculous. They should have thought about it. Some are going to be a little resistant. But if you want wins, this is the way to find wins. Yep, that's true. And real quick, let's just define FSI. Oh, yeah, Anthony, you want to know what that is. That stands for freestanding insert, which is the thing that is stuck in the middle of the newspaper, but is loose. Often used for, you know, the Walmart circular and the Target circular and the grocery store coupon piece. But you will see them used by direct response advertisers for both local and national. And interestingly, often the same copy put on an FSI versus run as an ad in the newspaper. The FSI will out pull the ad, often by a significant margin. And sometimes for the advertiser, there's remnant space available in the FSI when there isn't in the paper. And it can actually be a more economical thing as well. Better deal all around. That's great. Charles wants to know, Dan, how did you develop your multiple streams of income from each client you used your multiple... I must be reading this wrong. How do you develop your multiple streams of income from each client? I'm not just talking about copywriting. I'm primarily... Charles is primarily engineering oriented, but I have worked with marketing and salespeople for over 15 years. Well, ironically, in this case, his engineering background is useful. He's probably a handicapped and writing copy. And he's probably going to need more bubble baths. But Mike Fance, the dean at the Disney University used to say that all analytical people needed to take bubble baths. But the discipline of thinking that comes with the engineering background is really useful in the strategy side of all this. And so finding multiple streams of income that you can exploit for the client actually is not that difficult because most business owners of all kinds default to as few as one and usually no more than three primary means of attracting customers, of advertising, of marketing and selling. And they get very comfortable with that one, two, or three methods. And it becomes a box that they live in and they don't really do much experimentation with anything else. So almost always there's the opportunity to expand media horizontally. So if they're online, they might not be offline. If they're offline, they might not be online. If they're using print, they might not be using direct mail. If they're using postcards, they might not be using a Magalogue. If they're using a Magalogue, they might not be ever using postcards, etc., etc. So there's horizontal media expansion, right? Then even further, there's underuse of the primary asset of every business which is the customer itself. So also typically they have one, two, or three things they're selling to that customer. So you've got a restaurant, they sell them breakfast, lunch, dinner, and holiday dinners. So Christmas, Easter, whatever. But it's never occurred to them to be in the birthday party and retirement party business. And so they don't have packages, they don't have decor packages, they don't promote that. So almost every business has customers to whom additional things of their own making or brought in the door through alliances and joint ventures and sourcing can be sold to those customers that they are not doing at all. Sometimes you can go beyond consultant copywriter and carve yourself out a piece of such a business. Other times it's a great way to expand the amount of work that you get to do for the client by guiding them into these additional income streams readily available in their business. So I just sort of look at what media they use versus what media they aren't. And what are the one, two, or three, or four things that they're doing with the customer that they have and the things that they aren't, that perhaps they could be, and then begin to have those conversations. Okay, great. Next question, Chris Alflip wants to know, may we use the advertising critique with Dan Kennedy criteria as a template for our own critique? Just checking because, you know, because there is a copyright statement on the last page. Yeah, well, template, a better word would be inspiration or jumping off point. There's a copyright there for a reason. So nobody can take my name off of it, put their name on it and use it as a say. If they treat it really as a template, and everything's in the same order and same terminology, but just four of this instead of six of this, and that's still really not kosher. And the one that I gave you actually is in such substantial circulation, because it finds its way off into all of our members, that it's sort of like the risk is the same as telling somebody else's joke. If that joke teller is a very popular entertainer, some people are going to realize that you ripped off the joke. And so you really ought to take a look at that more as the basis being covered and the order of the basis are being covered in. And the internal psychology, which is very much about making the person aware of inadequacies in their current marketing, and then build your own. So you want to really use it as a jumping off point, not as something you tweak. Yep, that makes good. And everybody on this call can do that, certainly. Next, Alan, we have another question from Alan. After reviewing the wonderful information presented, I've put together a series of criteria from the session to define my optimum client market and segmentation as shown below. Am I missing something very important? Then he goes on to say, his optimum client characteristics are. The client's customers have a relatively high individual transaction cost, several thousands of dollars, and are not referral based businesses. There is a relatively large population of client types over 10,000, largely independent or working in small numbers in a business. They are business based, not individuals who do things. Due to the nature of their business, most constantly refresh a significant portion of their customer base. These are established businesses, not startups. Well, the first thing is he's done something very, very important. And that is that he's given some serious thought to a criteria. What makes a good client for him? And that may not necessarily define what makes a good client for you or for me. But there's some very smart things about the criteria that he has established. For example, that they're not in referral based businesses and that they need a constant flow of new clients. Which suggests that they have to do a lot of advertising and marketing, which means they need us. And they need us again and again and again. So he's done the right thing in general and he appears to have done some smart things. Am I missing something very important? Maybe. Not being Allen and not being thoroughly conversant with the clients he's working with and only spending a few minutes on this, not hours. Nothing really leaps out at me. The one thing that he didn't say is that I might add to his list that I would be curious about in finding out about different prospective clients within this group would be how aggressive they are now at investing in advertising and marketing. And so is there evidence of them doing smart things. I'd rather have somebody smart than somebody dumb personally. And of them doing a number of things. You don't want them doing everything perfectly and you don't want them doing everything because that's too good. But better that than somebody who doesn't appear to be spending any money. Because there's a momentum to everything. The person who's spending will spend. The person who's trying not to spend. It's harder to get them to spend. Other than that, there's nothing here that leaps out at me. And you know, the use of this now is in crafting everything that he does, everything that you do to speak to and resonate to the clients who fit that profile. Even to point of repelling those who don't. And so now the copy he writes about himself, the speech he gives, the information he has as a website, the title he puts on his free white paper that he gives away. All of that has to continually be held up next to the profile and really question, you know, if I build exactly the right date, am I using the right language and the right code words and the right promises for the person who fits this profile? That makes sense. Next, Melanie Rembrandt wants to know, many marketers advise having a free email newsletter to draw in people and grow a list of potential customers. What are your thoughts on offering a free newsletter for this reason? Or do you think it's wise to stick strictly to a pay for everything mentality and only offer a newsletter for a monthly fee in print version? Well, you could bust it up into five or six questions. But there's a place for free. And as she's observed, just about everybody does some of it. It also requires great caution about how much of it that you do. Because you can undermine positioning and or you can attract a lot of people that that don't fit the right profile and are not going to be a very productive use for you. I do think that not only do you want to limit it, but you do want a process to move from free to paid as early as possible and to continually be moving people from free to paid. And then the last sort of issue embedded in the question of electronic delivery versus print delivery. Look, you can sell electronic only. But if you really want to develop people from prospect to reader slash customer to client, you want them ascending in their relationship with you. You're going to do both. I mean, you're going to have online deliverables and you're going to have hard deliverables. And there's a real value perception issue in what appears to cost nothing to deliver. And it's the problem. The newspaper industry is wrestling with, the music industry is wrestling with, the e-book industry wrestles with. And so there's sort of this cultural free that since it doesn't cost anything to deliver, it ought not to cost much or in fact it ought to be free. Ignoring the cost of creation and ignoring the fact that the way it's being delivered really isn't the issue. It's what use is made of it is the issue. So I think you have to keep this very carefully restricted. And you do want to move people to pay one way or the other as early as you can, whether that's actual hard product or whether that's consulting or coaching or whatever. An initial consulting day in the client environment or half day or meeting or whatever, a bought and paid for product in the information marketing environment, which I suspect is really what she's asking this question about. A friend of mine who I wrote a book with who at one time was a famous or infamous whichever you prefer, Madam in New York, said all we ever gave away was a smile and a kind word. And so I mean everybody has something they give free, but you've got to be pretty careful about it. The other thing I will say about this is it matters a great deal what pond you're fishing in in the first place. So with highly qualified prospects, the problems related to free are less than with poorly qualified prospects. Because the highly qualified prospect first of all wants to get to the point of having things done for them as quickly as they can and can identify that this is a person who ought to be doing stuff for them. So free doesn't really undermine your positioning too much with that client. But in a much broader base of customers, you can really spoil and ruin them all by giving them too much free. Good. Okay. John Hughes wants to know, at some point you revealed you hand copied 500 sales letters. Howbert told you to do this. What 500 letters were they? I'd like to do this. Oh boy, why do you want to do this? Would you choose that differently? I don't think you'll like doing it, but it's a very useful exercise and it actually wasn't all letters. I mean, it adds any good direct response piece. So as long as you know it's a good one that it works, that it's a control, which you can go to archives for that. Sure. And you can start with your Hall of Fame book. We tell people that... Yeah, I forgot what it was called, but you have that big blue book, which is... Right. We tell people to do 10. I think maybe we should up it. I think... It actually doesn't matter because most won't do 10 or 100, so any number's okay. But no, I don't remember what I did and I've long since lost them, but I did do 500. Wow. And I did hand write them. And then the next part of the exercise is of course seeing if you can figure out a way that you would improve it if you were tweaking that control. But Halbert's tension, which I tend to agree with, is that there's a rhythm to copywriting. So there's mechanics, but then there's sort of an intangible rhythm that you get into and you only get there with experience. But synthetic experience is almost as good as and maybe in some respects better than actual experience. So this internalizes the rhythm you would gradually develop on your own through 500 actual assignments faster. And it's a quicker way to get better than doing it on the client's dimes over a period of years. I certainly never thought of it as fun. You know, I did about 10 a night, but I did do it. Dan, do you think reading them out loud would have the same impact or is it the hand brain? I think it is the hand. Florence Henderson's now deceased husband, a guy named Dr. John Kappus, who was a very skilled hypnotherapist. John had an entire system for goal setting and personal motivation that totally revolved around handwriting. And he had some quasi-clinical studies that actually demonstrated differences in comprehension, performance, all that, based on handwriting versus typing versus just reading. And John and I actually talked about it quite a bit when I shot an infomercial with him. And I do think there's some magic to it that is beyond my ability to explain. The reading aloud thing is interesting because that's a good way to test copy. Yes. And just the whole breath and, you know, if you stumble and what stops you. And that rhythm, you know, it has to have a rhythm. Okay, here's a question from David. Dan, will you consider offering some form of a coaching program similar to the very impressive program you offered at the Academy to us alumni in the next year or so? I hope the answer is yes. Well, you won't see me. I mean, you won't see me overtly do it. If enough people ask for it, yeah. Okay. That makes sense. And you can either ask Dan directly or you can certainly get a message to us and we will pass it along. Yeah, I mean, that will be sort of weightless driven. Okay. Let's see. I think that was all the questions that we marked. Yeah, I think so. So, you know, we could go live if you would like. Okay. Denise? All right, ladies and gentlemen, what I'm going to do is open the lines in a moment. If you are interested in asking Dan a question, what I would ask you to do is simply state your first name. Several of you will be speaking at once. I will use my best ears to capture a name. I will then announce that name and that person can ask the question and I will repeat the process. So, at this point the lines are open. If you have a question, please state your first name. Donald. All right, Donald, your first up. Go ahead and ask your question, please. Well, I enjoyed the session very much and, you know, following up on the Renegade Millionaire program you sent us. You said that was done around 2004 and you flat out said that if you had to pick one direct marketing, well, one marketing weapon of all, it would be direct mail. I would be curious if you believe that now, 2010. Absolutely. I, and it's very interesting in that at the moment I have a lot of working house from three clients who, two of them do a great deal of their advertising marketing and actually selling online and one does a great deal in broadcast, TV and radio. And they're well-established companies. They originally built their business with direct mail and gradually got away from it and more and more and more and relied more and more on electronic media and they are eagerly finding their way back to more and more direct mail. And there's a lot of reasons for it, but one is really the ability to exercise a great deal of control by precise targeting and list segmentation and message to market matching and all of that that is much more difficult to do with any other media. Secondly, in making significant sized dollar amount sales to a given market and getting attention to a complex sales business. There is even a sense of obligation to a mail piece, especially an elaborate one, that the client, that the recipient understands somebody spent money to put in their mail as opposed to media that appears to be free to them. And last, it's the closest you can come to the face-to-face sale where while they're paying attention, they are paying attention only to it. But the precise targeting is really the biggest issue. I mean, we're just able to hone in on somebody about which we know a great deal and match the message to that person in a way that's very difficult to do any plus sales. Last, there's the quality of customers. So today was my coaching class, so I was on the phone with a client earlier today. And a great deal of testing in the last three months with online lead generation, print lead generation, and direct mail lead generation. By far the most expensive lead is the lead being created by direct mail, but the most profitable, but the most net profitable customer is also the customer being achieved by direct mail. So the value slash quality difference is disproportionately better, greater than is the added cost. So no, my opinion has not altered. Okay. Denise, next question. All right, Chuck, I think you had a question. Yes, thanks, Denise. Dan, thanks very much for the follow-up conference call here. The Academy was fantastic, and I'm putting everything to work as we speak right now. But I have a question. Right, thanks, Chuck. And maybe it's too big for the call, but I just wanted to get your idea on how you structure a retainer deal with a client, specifically how much time payments per period, what would you commit to such an arrangement in advance? Would you say dollars per quarter or your entire X amount of my time or so forth and so on? But how would you structure something like that? Well, you've put your finger on the difficulty, of course, is, you know, clients tend to engage in mission claims under any circumstances, but especially in a retainer agreement. So the real key is the most precise and detailed definition possible of what they're going to get and what they're entitled to and what they aren't. And it's never possible, at least I've never found it possible, to really get that to point of perfection. I mean, so for example, with the retainer type clients that I have, I mean, the access issue is, it really boils down to what I call the reasonable man standard. Which I get to decide what's reasonable and my remedy is, of course, terminating the relationship. The control really is more because they have to go through a process to get to me than it is anything else, including contract. So some discipline is imposed on them. But I have three or four of these right now and I'm in the process of working out a $10,000 a month arrangement with a new client, with the retainer against percentages of results. And we've gone back and forth a couple of times. Both of us eager to get the definition right to avoid conflict and frustration, you know, in the future. So it really is about limitations on work product and limitations on time and limitations on access. And those are the three areas, I think, that you have to do as good a job as you possibly can of having mutual understanding gotten into writing. I think there's a bigger umbrella issue, too, which really is who perceives they need, who more. And an overall sense of, you know, respect. And so, you know, if they sort of understand your general philosophical approach to the business and they have respect for you, then you won't suffer a lot of abuse. And if the mission creep starts to occur or abusive access starts to occur, usually it can be rained in. But there's no doubt it's tricky. Thank you, Dan. We are close to time, but let's see if we have any other questions. Yeah, I think we can squeeze in maybe two more, Denise. All right. Anyone else if you have a question, please state your first name. Do I have a first name? Shannon. Shannon? Shannon. Shannon, go right ahead, Shannon. Hey, Dan, how you doing? I'm good, how are you? I'm terrific. Hey, listen, I just had a question about authoring books. I know you've got tons of them out there. And my question is, do you yourself have any kind of guideline of how you decide whether you would go to find an agent to publish your book, or whether you should just self-publish it yourself? Is there any sort of like, you know, guidance that you use, whether to publish yourself or whether you should find an agent? Well, yeah, and it's to be simplistic. Okay. So, you self-publish for profit, you trade publish for PR. Beautiful. It's a little more complicated than that. Okay. I mean, so there's issues, there's big issues of distribution. And distribution, muscle. So, I mean, these days, there's no difference really in, I mean, everybody can get their book on Amazon. So you may not be able to get your self-published book on the shelf of every Barnes and Noble and keep it there. But you can be an Amazon. Anybody can be an Amazon. But what you probably can't get done, except in the Catch-22 of after your book is selling like hotcakes, for example, is entrepreneur press has a really good relationship with Amazon. And so, like for my wealth book that's out now, there's been one day of banner ads at Amazon. There's been one dedicated email, and Amazon's about to do another dedicated email to all of their marketing category book buyers. And that requires some clout to get that done. So, if you view the book by PR, I mean, self-promotion, marketing, fishing expedition, prominence, etc., then that kind of clout matters, you know, a lot. Very hard to get your book in an airport bookstore if you're self-published. A publisher can get it done. You know, etc. So, those are the reasons that have driven much of my output to quote-quote real publishers. And really to get those deals, you're best served by having a good agent. Not to say that you can't go over the transom because you can. My first deals were because I got rejections from agents, so I just went and did it myself. But now, if you want profit, then, and you have a means of selling them, then you self-publish because you trade away a lot of dough for that distribution and that distribution muscle. If you're prolific enough, then you can play both games, which I have my entire life. Okay? Yeah, thanks. You're welcome. If there is one more Denise, we can squeeze them in. Okay, one more. Please state your first name. Donald. Donald? Right, but now let's let us, gentlemen, since the last question. Donald is a second-time questionnaire, but he is eager and ready.
